title: Class & Static Methods
summary: Beyond instance methods: @classmethod for alternative constructors and @staticmethod for related helpers.

blocks.md: Most methods you've written are **instance methods** — they take `self` and act on one object. Python has two more kinds, marked with decorators: **class methods** (`@classmethod`, receive the class as `cls`) and **static methods** (`@staticmethod`, receive nothing special).

blocks.caption: all three kinds
blocks.output:
Pizza with tomato, mozzarella, basil
True

blocks.md: Because a class method receives `cls`, it can build and return a new instance — a clean way to offer named constructors like `Pizza.margherita()`. Using `cls` (not the literal class name) means subclasses get the right type automatically.

blocks.title: Which one to use?
blocks.md: **Instance method** — needs the object's data (`self`). **Class method** — needs the class but not a specific instance (factories, config). **Static method** — a plain function that just *belongs* with the class for organization; it touches neither `self` nor `cls`.

blocks.question: What does a @classmethod receive as its first argument?
blocks.explanation: A class method receives the class as `cls`. That lets it act on the class (e.g. build and return a new instance) without needing an existing object, and it respects subclasses.

keyTakeaways: Instance methods take `self` and act on one object.
`@classmethod` takes `cls` (the class) — great for alternative constructors like `Pizza.margherita()`.
`@staticmethod` takes no special first argument — a helper grouped with the class.
Use `cls(...)` in class methods so subclasses build the correct type.

exercise.prompt: Add a `@classmethod` called `from_string` to a `Point` class that parses "3,4" into `Point(3, 4)`. The class stores `self.x` and `self.y`.
exercise.hint: Split the text on ',', convert each part to int, and return cls(x, y).
